revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:飛鳥の石造物]]
{{Refimprove|date=June 25, 2019|qq=}} '''Stone Structures of Asuka''' (Asuka no Sekizōbutsu) refer to stone relics and structures created during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} in the [[Asuka region]] of {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, centered on present-day {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|qid=Q752397|lt=Asuka Village|1=Asuka, Nara}} and its surroundings. == Overview == Many of Asuka’s stone structures are made of {{ill|Granite|en|Granite|qid=Q41177|lt=Granite|1=Granite}}. The purpose of these structures is often unclear, and their designs and representations are unlike the later mainstream {{ill|Buddhist art|en|Buddhist art|qid=Q1001041|lt=Buddhist art|1=Buddhist art}} of Japan. Due to their mysterious nature, they are often referred to as enigmatic stone structures.<ref>Kyōtarō Nishikawa (Supervision), Toshifumi Konno (Editor), ''Traveling through Buddhist Statues: Nara'', Shibundō, 1991, p. 202.</ref> Some interpretations suggest a connection to [[Taoism]].<ref>Koji Fukunaga, Minoru Chida, and Toru Takahashi, ''Japanese Taoist Sites'', Asahi Shimbun, 1987, pp. 19–24.</ref> == Main Stone Structures == ; Saruishi (Monkey Stones, Shimoda-hira, Asuka Village) [[File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|thumb|Saruishi, "Sannō Gongen"]] {{Main|Saruishi|qq=}} : Excavated in {{ill|1702|en|1702|qid=Q6819|lt=1702|1=1702}} (Genroku 15) from a rice field west of the Memyama Kofun. ; Stone Human Statue [[File:Sekijinzou (Ishigami-iseki).jpg|thumb|Stone Human Statue]] : Also known as the ''Dōsojin'' (guardian deities of roads). Unearthed in {{ill|1903|en|1903|qid=Q2045|lt=1903|1=1903}} (Meiji 36) from the same site as the Shumisen Stone, the statue depicts a [[male]] seated on a rock, with a {{ill|Female|en|Female|qid=Q43445|lt=Female|1=Female}} figure standing behind him with her hands placed on his shoulders. Narrow pipes run through the statue from the man’s feet to his mouth, branching toward the woman’s mouth, suggesting it was part of a {{ill|fountain|en|fountain|qid=Q483453|lt=fountain|1=Fountain}} system. Their attire is of Asuka-period style, but their facial features have led to theories of influences from [[:en:Persia|Persia]] or {{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}. A reconstructed fountain version is on display at the {{ill|Asuka Historical Museum|en|Asuka Historical Museum|qid=Q4811853|lt=Asuka Historical Museum|1=Asuka Historical Museum}}. ; Jintōseki (Human Head Stone, Kōgaku-ji Temple, Takatori Town) : Also called ''Kaoishi'' (Face Stone). Found in the temple grounds, this carved stone face is believed to have been unearthed with the Saruishi stones. ; Nimenishi (Two-Faced Stone, Tachibana, Asuka Village) [[File:Tatibanatera Nimenseki 01.jpg|thumb|Nimenishi]] : A dual-faced stone statue located in the grounds of [[Tachibana Temple]]. ; Kameishi (Turtle Stone, Kawahara, Asuka Village) [[File:Kameishi.JPG|thumb|Kameishi]] {{Main|Kameishi|qq=}} : A giant stone resembling a crouching {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|qid=Q223044|lt=Turtle|1=Turtle}}. ; Kamegata Sekizōbutsu (Turtle-Shaped Stone Structure) [[File:KameAndKoban.jpg|thumb|Turtle-Shaped Stone Structure]] {{Main|Sakafuneishi Ruins#Turtle-Shaped Stone Structure|qq=}} : Discovered in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=1998|1=1998}} (Heisei 10) during an excavation near the Sakafuneishi site. ; Maraiseki (Phallus Stone, Iwaya, Asuka Village) [[File:Maraisi 01.jpg|thumb|Maraiseki]] : A stone resembling a {{ill|Phallus|en|Phallus|qid=Q168552|lt=Phallus|1=Phallus}} near the entrance to the [[Asuka Historical National Park]] in the Iwaya district. ; Mirokuishi (Maitreya Stone) [[File:Mirokuishi 1864.jpg|thumb|Mirokuishi]] {{Main|Mirokuishi|qq=}} : Located on the east bank of the [[Asuka River]] southwest of [[Asukadera Temple]]. ; Shumisen Stone (Mount Meru Stone) [[File:Shumisenseki (Ishigami-iseki).jpg|thumb|Shumisen Stone]] : Discovered in {{ill|1902|en|1902|qid=Q2043|lt=1902|1=1902}} (Meiji 35) in a rice field in Ishigami, Asuka Village. Believed to represent Mount Meru. ; Sakafuneishi (Sake Ship Stone, Oka, Asuka Village) [[File:SakafuneIshi.jpg|thumb|Sakafuneishi]] {{Main|Sakafuneishi Ruins|qq=}} : A geometric stone basin believed to have ritual purposes. ; Izumi Sakafuneishi (Izumi Sake Ship Stone) {{Main|Sakafuneishi Ruins#Izumi Sakafuneishi|qq=}} : Unearthed in {{ill|1916|en|1916|qid=Q2087|lt=1916|1=1916}} (Taishō 5) from a rice field. Currently part of the Nomura Garden in Kyoto. ; Kurumaseki (Wheel Stone) : Found near the Sakafuneishi site in {{ill|1935|en|1935|qid=Q18658|lt=1935|1=1935}} (Shōwa 10). ; Mon’yōseki (Patterned Stone, Toyoura, Asuka Village) : Located in the ruins of Toyoura Temple. ; Oni no Manaita/Oni no Sekichin (Demon’s Cutting Board/Demon’s Toilet) [[File:Oni no Manaita.JPG|thumb|Demon’s Cutting Board]] {{Main|Oni no Manaita and Oni no Sekichin|qq=}} : Mysterious stones in Noguchi and Hirata, Asuka Village. ; Masuda no Iwafune (Masuda’s Rock Ship, Kashihara City) [[File:Masuda-no-Iwafune-2-2.jpg|thumb|Masuda no Iwafune]] {{Main|Masuda no Iwafune|qq=}} : The largest stone structure among the Asuka relics, designated a historic site by Nara Prefecture. {{Clear|qq=}} == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * ''Asuka Historical Museum Catalog 35: Stone Structures of Asuka'' (2000) * {{Cite book|author=Kunihiko Kawakami|year=2003|title=Digging into Asuka|series=Kodansha Sensho Mechie|publisher=Kodansha|isbn=4-06-258258-9|ref={{SfnRef|Kawakami|2003|qq=}}|qq=}} * Kawakami, Kunihiko, ''The Asuka Excavation Chronicles'', Fusosha, 2004. * Special Issue of ''Taiyō'', ''Asuka: Journey into the Ancient Past'', Heibonsha, 2005.
== See Also == * [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]] * [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] == External Links == * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAISI/asukaisi.html Asuka Historical Museum - Stone Structures of Asuka] {{Japanese-history-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|飛鳥の石造物|version=102073675|comment=Imported full ja history}}
